metadata.summary: LCEL pipes and LangGraph state machines for building production AI chains and agents with durable state, checkpointing, and human-in-the-loop.

content.title: LangChain and LangGraph: Building Production AI Agents
content.summary: LangChain (LCEL pipe syntax) and LangGraph (state machine framework) for building production AI chains and agents. LangChain handles standard tool-calling agents via `create_react_agent()` and composable pipelines via `prompt | model | parser`. LangGraph adds durable state, checkpointing, human-in-the-loop interrupts, and multi-agent Supervisor patterns for complex control flow.

why.p: LangChain provides provider-agnostic wiring, built-in retry/fallback, and LangSmith observability out of the box. LangGraph's state machine model solves the hardest agent problems: surviving process restarts, pausing for human approval before irreversible actions, and debugging non-linear workflows via time-travel replay.

list.item: Building multi-step AI workflows where each step's output feeds the next (LCEL pipe chains)
Standard ReAct tool-calling agent with 3-10 tools — `create_react_agent()` covers 80% of use cases
Complex control flow requiring state machines: human-in-the-loop approval, conditional branching, retry logic (LangGraph)
Long-running workflows that must survive process restarts — LangGraph checkpointing persists state to disk/DB
Multi-agent orchestration: Supervisor → Worker patterns with role specialization
RAG pipelines requiring custom retrieval → reranking → synthesis chains
Single LLM call — use the provider SDK directly (anthropic, openai); LangChain adds 50-200ms overhead
Document-centric RAG with complex index types — LlamaIndex has better RAG abstractions
Browser or desktop agents — LangChain's tools don't wrap browser automation well; use Playwright directly
Simple prompt templates — Python f-strings or Jinja2 avoid the dependency
High-throughput inference (greater than 100 RPS) — LangChain's Python overhead becomes measurable; use vLLM or direct SDK

section.name: lcel-fundamentals
section.p: LCEL (LangChain Expression Language) provides a syntax for composing chains using Python's pipe operator.

rule.statement: Use `langchain-core` only in production agents, not the full `langchain` package — core is stable; full langchain pulls 200+ transitive dependencies.
rule.rationale: Dependency bloat creates version conflicts and larger container images. Core contains everything needed for LCEL chains and structured output.

rule.statement: Use `.with_structured_output(PydanticModel)` instead of output parsers; structured output is enforced by the API, not fragile string parsing.
rule.rationale: String parsers break silently when the model changes its output format. Structured output fails with a clear error that is easier to debug.

example.title: LCEL pipe chain patterns
example.code:
# Basic chain chain = prompt | model | StrOutputParser()
# With structured output chain = prompt | model.with_structured_output(MySchema)
# With retry on transient errors chain = (prompt | model | parser).with_retry(stop_after_attempt=3)
# With fallback model chain = primary_chain.with_fallbacks([fallback_chain])

antipattern.description: Using `.with_retry()` on chains without distinguishing transient errors from logic errors.
antipattern.reason: Blind retry amplifies costs without fixing root cause. Rate limit errors need backoff; logic errors need prompt fixes, not retries.

section.name: chain-patterns
section.p: Common architectural patterns for composing chains: Sequential, Router, MapReduce, Fallback, and Parallel execution.

Sequential: `prompt | model | parser`. Router: classifier → branch to chain_a or chain_b. MapReduce: `[doc1, doc2] → map → reduce`. Fallback: `primary_chain.with_fallbacks([fallback])`. RunnableParallel: run independent chains concurrently.

rule.statement: Test chains by invoking each component individually before wiring with `|`; isolates bugs before they compound in full chain traces.
rule.rationale: LCEL `|` produces hard-to-debug stack traces when intermediate steps fail; error messages point to internal chain internals rather than your code.

section.name: observability
section.p: Understanding what happens inside chains is critical for debugging and production reliability.

rule.statement: Enable LangSmith tracing from day 1 on new projects; retrofitting observability into existing chains is painful.
rule.rationale: LangSmith traces every `|` step with full input/output. Without it, debugging unexpected chain outputs requires print-statement archaeology.

rule.statement: Disable LangSmith tracing (`LANGCHAIN_TRACING_V2=false`) in latency-critical paths; tracing adds ~30ms per chain call.
rule.rationale: At high throughput, 30ms per call compounds. In a 10-step chain, tracing adds 300ms — measurable in user-facing endpoints.

section.name: langgraph-state-management
section.p: LangGraph extends LCEL with state machines, checkpointing, and human-in-the-loop capabilities.

rule.statement: Store LangGraph state as flat JSON-serializable dicts — nested custom objects silently fail serialization in checkpoint backends.
rule.rationale: Checkpoint backends (PostgreSQL, Redis) require JSON serialization. Custom Python objects cause silent serialization failures; the state appears to persist but is actually lost on process restart.

rule.statement: Always set `max_iterations` on agents using `RunnableConfig` or graph loop bounds — the default is unlimited.
rule.rationale: `create_react_agent()` has no built-in iteration cap. Agents loop indefinitely on ambiguous tasks without custom `stop_sequence` or `max_iterations` config.

example.title: Minimal LangGraph agent with persistence
example.code:
from langgraph.prebuilt import create_react_agent from langgraph.checkpoint.memory import MemorySaver
checkpointer = MemorySaver() agent = create_react_agent(model, tools, checkpointer=checkpointer)
config = {"configurable": {"thread_id": "session-123"}} result = agent.invoke({"messages": [("user", task)]}, config=config)

section.name: conditional-edges-and-routing
section.p: Routing between graph nodes enables conditional control flow and multi-agent patterns.

rule.statement: Use `with_structured_output(PydanticModel)` for routing decisions in conditional edge functions; return the next node name as a typed field.
rule.rationale: String parsing of free-text routing decisions fails silently when the model produces unexpected phrasing. Structured output enforces exact node name values.

section.name: human-in-the-loop
section.p: Interrupts allow humans to approve or override decisions before irreversible actions are taken.

rule.statement: Use `interrupt_before=["action_node"]` for nodes that perform irreversible actions (send email, write to DB, call payment API); never rely on agent self-restraint.
rule.rationale: Agents cannot undo executed side effects. A misrouted task dispatched to a worker that calls an external API is irreversible without a human checkpoint.

section.name: multi-agent-supervisor-pattern
section.p: The Supervisor pattern coordinates multiple worker agents with a central routing node.

rule.statement: Use `Annotated[list, operator.add]` for accumulated results fields in SupervisorState; direct list assignment discards previous worker outputs.
rule.rationale: LangGraph merges partial state updates with the accumulator. Plain list assignment replaces the list on every update, causing earlier worker results to be lost.

section.name: deprecated-patterns
section.p: LangChain has evolved rapidly. Older patterns are still documented but should not be used in new projects.

antipattern.description: Using LLMChain, AgentExecutor, ConversationChain, or SequentialChain from legacy LangChain.
antipattern.reason: These are deprecated but still widely documented on Stack Overflow. Agents trained on web data will suggest them. Use LCEL equivalents: `prompt | model | parser`, `create_react_agent`, `RunnableWithMessageHistory`, LCEL pipe chain.

antipattern.description: LCEL operator (`|`) produces hard-to-debug stack traces when intermediate steps fail; error messages point to internal chain internals, not your code.
antipattern.reason: Always test components individually first to isolate bugs.

antipattern.description: `create_react_agent()` has no built-in iteration cap — agents loop indefinitely on ambiguous tasks without custom `stop_sequence` or max_iterations config.
LangGraph state serialization fails silently for custom non-serializable objects in state.
antipattern.reason: Always use JSON-serializable types in StateGraph.

antipattern.description: LangSmith tracing adds ~30ms latency per chain call.

section.name: async-and-threading
section.p: LangChain supports async execution but has pitfalls when mixing sync and async code.

antipattern.description: Using a custom `CallbackHandler` with blocking I/O in an async chain.
antipattern.reason: LangChain callbacks execute synchronously in async chains by default. A blocking callback stalls the entire chain. Use `AsyncCallbackHandler` instead.

antipattern.description: Using `batch()` with tools that are not thread-safe.
antipattern.reason: LCEL `batch()` runs in a thread pool by default. Non-thread-safe tools cause race conditions. Use `abatch()` with async tools instead.

antipattern.description: `create_react_agent` internally uses `MessagesState` — adding custom state fields requires a custom graph, not the prebuilt agent.
antipattern.reason: If you need custom state, define your own StateGraph instead of extending create_react_agent.

section.name: tool-routing-and-selection
section.p: Agents select and call tools based on descriptions. Poor descriptions lead to wrong tool selection.

antipattern.description: Providing ambiguous or overlapping tool descriptions without testing tool selection explicitly.
antipattern.reason: The LLM reads tool descriptions verbatim. Ambiguous descriptions cause the agent to select wrong tools without error. Wrong tool selection fails silently.

rule.statement: Test tool routing explicitly: invoke the agent with a sample query, inspect which tool was selected, and verify it's the expected one.
rule.rationale: Tool selection errors compound silently through the rest of the pipeline.

section.name: security
section.p: Agents that retrieve external content or take user input need input sanitization.

rule.statement: Sanitize user input before injecting into prompts; validate retrieved content before including in RAG chains to prevent prompt injection.
rule.rationale: A malicious document in the corpus or a crafted user input can override agent instructions. Retrieved content must be treated as untrusted data, not trusted instructions.

list.item: Use `langchain-core` only (not `langchain`) for production agents — core is stable; full langchain pulls 200+ transitive deps
Always set `max_iterations` on agents using `RunnableConfig` or graph loop bounds — default is unlimited
Use `.with_structured_output(PydanticModel)` instead of output parsers — structured output is enforced by the API, not fragile string parsing
Store LangGraph state as flat JSON-serializable dicts — nested custom objects silently fail serialization in checkpoint backends
Test chains by invoking each component individually before wiring with `|` — isolates bugs before they compound in full chain traces
Enable LangSmith from day 1 on new projects; retrofitting observability into existing chains is painful

section.name: agentic-workflow
section.p: Integrating LangChain into multi-agent systems requires careful design of subagent responsibilities.

rule.statement: Use `create_react_agent(model, tools, checkpointer=MemorySaver())` for simple agent tasks driven by Claude subagents.
rule.rationale: This covers the common case and requires minimal boilerplate.

rule.statement: For complex workflows, define a LangGraph `StateGraph` where each node is a Claude subagent call with a specific role (planner, executor, critic).
rule.rationale: This separates concerns and makes the workflow explicit and testable.

rule.statement: Route between nodes with `add_conditional_edges` based on structured output from the previous node.
rule.rationale: Structured routing prevents silent failures from ambiguous or unparseable outputs.

rule.statement: Use `interrupt_before` for human-in-the-loop checkpoints at high-risk action nodes.
rule.rationale: This prevents irreversible actions without human approval.

rule.statement: Chain observability through LangSmith by setting `LANGCHAIN_TRACING_V2=true` — every subagent call is automatically traced with full input/output.
rule.rationale: This provides visibility into the entire workflow for debugging and optimization.

template.purpose: Supervisor pattern with conditional edge routing and state accumulation
